notes.note: The Epicureans held that pleasure is the highest good and counselled withdrawal from public life. Cicero mock-laments that a serious jurist has adopted a creed that, taken literally, undermines the whole basis of civil law and public duty.

notes.anchor: as it ought to be done well between honest men
notes.note: A famous clause of the legal action on a fiduciary trust (fiducia): 'ut inter bonos bene agier oportet.' Cicero's pun is that an Epicurean, who acts only for his own pleasure, can never be the 'honest man' (bonus) the formula presupposes.

notes.anchor: the action for partitioning common property
notes.note: The actio communi dividundo, the lawsuit by which co-owners divided jointly held property. Cicero jokes that 'common' property is meaningless to a sect that measures everything by private pleasure.

The oath 'per Iovem Lapidem' was the most solemn Roman oath, sworn holding a flint stone sacred to Jupiter. Cicero's barb: how can Trebatius administer this oath when Epicurean theology denies that the gods feel anger and so could punish a perjurer?

Ulubrae was a byword for a tiny, all-but-abandoned town in the Pomptine marshes. Cicero turns Trebatius's Epicurean withdrawal from politics into a comic crisis for these notional 'constituents,' picking up the frog joke of Fam. 7.18.
